title: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
oneLiner: Autoimmune thrombophilia causing recurrent venous/arterial thrombosis and pregnancy loss; often missed until second event or catastrophic presentation.

sections.Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS): **One-liner:** Autoimmune thrombophilia causing recurrent venous/arterial thrombosis and pregnancy loss; often missed until second event or catastrophic presentation.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Catastrophic APS (CAPS):** Acute, widespread microvascular thrombosis leading to multi-organ failure (kidneys, lungs, brain, skin).  
  *Key finding:* Rapid onset of thrombotic storm (≥3 organs in days) ± livedo racemosa, thrombocytopenia, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA) — **not routine DVT/PE**.
- **Massive PE / stroke:** APS often presents with unprovoked DVT or stroke.  
  *Key finding:* Sudden pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, haemoptysis, or new focal neurological deficit with **no alternative cause** (e.g., atrial fibrillation, sepsis).  
  *Rule out immediately:* CT pulmonary angiogram or CT head + thrombophilia screen **before** starting empiric anticoagulation.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Unexplained venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) — especially recurrent or at unusual sites (cerebral sinus, hepatic, renal, portal)
  - Arterial thrombosis in young patient (<50 years) — stroke, TIA, myocardial infarction, limb ischaemia
  - Recurrent early pregnancy loss (≥3, or ≥1 late loss >10 weeks)
  - Premature pre-eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, placental insufficiency
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Signs of DVT (calf swelling, warmth, Homan's sign — though unreliable)
  - Livedo racemosa (reticulated, non-blanching purplish rash on trunk/limbs)
  - Splinter haemorrhages, digital ischaemia, skin ulcers (suggestive of microvascular occlusion)
  - Hypertension ± proteinuria (if renal artery thrombosis or pre-eclampsia)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Inherited thrombophilia** (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation): family history, younger age, usually no pregnancy loss pattern — differentiate with genetic testing.
- **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT):** thrombocytopenia, timing after heparin use, positive serotonin release assay; **splitter:** if on heparin, check platelet count and functional assay.
- **Paradoxical embolism (PFO):** young with cryptogenic stroke — no thrombophilia markers; echo with bubble study.
- **Vasculitis (SLE, Behçet's):** systemic inflammation, oral/genital ulcers, serositis, positive ANCA/ANA — splitter: thrombocytopenia + livedo points to APS; complement low suggests SLE.
- **Malignancy-related thrombosis:** weight loss, anaemia, thrombocytosis, age >50; CT chest/abdo/pelvis if unexplained.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (if chest pain: look for RV strain in PE)
  - Urine dipstick (proteinuria, haematuria)
  - Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age
  - Observe for livedo racemosa
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (thrombocytopenia common), coagulation screen (prolonged aPTT if lupus anticoagulant present), LFTs, U&Es, CRP, ESR
  - Thrombophilia screen: **lupus anticoagulant** (dilute Russell viper venom time, aPTT with mixing studies), **anticardiolipin antibodies** (IgG/IgM), **anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies**
  - *Note:* Warfarin/heparin can interfere with lupus anticoagulant testing; take blood before starting anticoagulation if possible
  - If CAPS suspected: LDH, haptoglobin, schistocytes on blood film (MAHA), troponin, lactate
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Doppler ultrasound for DVT; CT pulmonary angiogram for PE; CT/MR venography for cerebral sinus thrombosis
  - **Second-line:** Echocardiogram (valve lesions, Libman-Sacks endocarditis), MRI brain if neurological symptoms
- **Diagnostic criteria (Revised Sapporo criteria — used for research but guides clinical diagnosis):**
  - **Clinical:** ≥1 episode of thrombosis (any site, any vessel) **or** pregnancy morbidity (≥3 early losses, ≥1 late loss >10 weeks, ≥1 preterm delivery <34 weeks due to pre-eclampsia/placental insufficiency)
  - **Laboratory:** Persistent moderate/high-titre anticardiolipin or anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies, or lupus anticoagulant detected on ≥2 occasions at least 12 weeks apart
- **Never-miss test:** **Thrombophilia screen (including lupus anticoagulant) BEFORE starting anticoagulation** — if already on warfarin, test when off (e.g., bridging with LMWH) or check IgG/IgM antibodies as they are less affected.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute thrombosis (DVT/PE): start therapeutic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily or 1 mg/kg BD) — **do not delay for test results** if high suspicion
  - If suspected CAPS: **urgent senior/haematology involvement**, start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily × 3 days), IVIG (1 g/kg daily × 2 days), and consider plasma exchange
  - If stroke/TIA without large vessel occlusion: same LMWH, then transition to warfarin; avoid OCP, smoking
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with repeat antibodies after 12 weeks
  - Start warfarin once LMWH therapeutic (overlap for ≥5 days until INR therapeutic)
  - Target INR 2.0–3.0 for venous thrombosis; **consider target 3.0–4.0 if arterial thrombosis or recurrent events on conventional INR** — discuss with haematologist
  - For pregnancy: low-dose aspirin ± LMWH (avoid warfarin) — refer to obstetric medicine
  - Avoid DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban etc.) in triple-positive APS (lupus anticoagulant + both anti-cardiolipin & anti-β2) — increased thrombotic risk; use warfarin only
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (except single provoked VTE with low-titre antibodies — discuss individualised)
  - Monitor INR weekly until stable, then monthly
  - Address modifiable risk factors: control BP, lipids, avoid OCP/HRT, stop smoking
  - Regular follow-up at rheumatology/haematology clinic
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance if stable; avoid dehydration (prothrombotic)
  - VTE prophylaxis: **not indicated** in APS itself (already on therapeutic anticoagulation); if not yet started — use LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) only if low bleeding risk and no current thrombosis
  - Diet/NBM: nil if stroke with dysphagia, otherwise oral diet
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; more frequent if unstable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **LMWH (enoxaparin):** therapeutic dose 1.5 mg/kg SC daily (or 1 mg/kg BD) — for acute thrombosis
  - **Warfarin:** loading dose 5–10 mg PO daily for 2 days, then adjust according to INR; typical maintenance 2–6 mg daily
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Unfractionated heparin (IV with aPTT monitoring) if LMWH contraindicated (e.g., severe renal impairment)
  - Fondaparinux (if HIT history)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - DOACs: avoid in triple-positive APS (increased risk of stroke); use only if low-titre, single negative lupus anticoagulant — haematology advice
  - NSAIDs + anticoagulation: increased bleeding risk
  - Antibiotics (e.g., metronidazole, fluconazole) potentiate warfarin — check INR frequently
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (1 mg/kg once daily) or switch to unfractionated heparin
  - Warfarin: no renal adjustment; caution in hepatic impairment (check baseline INR)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting warfarin without LMWH cover in acute thrombosis** — risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis (due to protein C depletion) and paradoxical embolism from unstable clot
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Catastrophic APS (CAPS) — mortality 30–50% if untreated
  - Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) — rare but possible; check platelets before heparin and every 2–3 days
  - Bleeding (especially if supratherapeutic INR) — reverse warfarin with vitamin K (1–5 mg PO/IV) or fresh frozen plasma if major bleed
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent thrombosis despite anticoagulation (check compliance, INR variability, consider target INR 3–4)
  - Accelerated atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction
  - Chronic kidney disease from renal artery thrombosis or microangiopathy
  - Cognitive decline (microvascular strokes)
  - Non-thrombotic manifestations: thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, valve disease (Libman-Sacks)
sections.Prognosis: - With appropriate anticoagulation, 5-year survival >90% for uncomplicated APS. Catastrophic APS has 30–50% mortality. Recurrence risk 10–20% at 5 years if INR subtherapeutic. Pregnancy outcomes good with LMWH + aspirin (live birth >80%).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always repeat antibody testing after 12 weeks** to confirm persistent positivity — single positive can be transient (e.g., infection, drug-induced).
- **In pregnancy**, start low-dose aspirin (75 mg daily) before conception, and add LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg daily or BD) once pregnancy confirmed — continue until 6 weeks postpartum.
- **Use the "APS score"** (Global APS Score) to stratify thrombotic risk (combines antibody profile, clinical history) — helps guide intensity of anticoagulation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT prescribe DOACs in triple-positive APS** (lupus anticoagulant + both anticardiolipin and anti-β2 antibodies) — increased risk of stroke.
- **Do NOT wait for antibody results to start anticoagulation** in acute thrombosis with high clinical suspicion — treat first, test later.
- **Do NOT stop warfarin for minor procedures** without haematology advice — bridging with LMWH is often needed because of high thrombotic risk.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening chest pain, shortness of breath, haemoptysis (PE)
  - New severe headache, stroke-like symptoms (cerebral sinus thrombosis)
  - Signs of bleeding (overt haemorrhage, melaena, haematuria, easy bruising)
  - Unexplained fever, confusion, hypotension (possible CAPS)
- **Follow-up:** Haematology clinic within 4–6 weeks for INR check/antibody repeat; rheumatology clinic if associated SLE. Anticoagulation review with community pharmacist (e.g., INR monitoring).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Suspected CAPS (organ failure, MAHA, rapid deterioration)
  - Any thrombosis on anticoagulation (breakthrough event)
  - Major bleeding (haemodynamic instability, fall in Hb >20 g/L)
  - Any pregnant woman with new thrombosis or obstetric complication
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <13 seizures, or focal neurology
  - Massive PE with shock or cardiac arrest

rawMarkdown:
## Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS)

**One-liner:** Autoimmune thrombophilia causing recurrent venous/arterial thrombosis and pregnancy loss; often missed until second event or catastrophic presentation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Catastrophic APS (CAPS):** Acute, widespread microvascular thrombosis leading to multi-organ failure (kidneys, lungs, brain, skin).  
  *Key finding:* Rapid onset of thrombotic storm (≥3 organs in days) ± livedo racemosa, thrombocytopenia, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA) — **not routine DVT/PE**.
- **Massive PE / stroke:** APS often presents with unprovoked DVT or stroke.  
  *Key finding:* Sudden pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, haemoptysis, or new focal neurological deficit with **no alternative cause** (e.g., atrial fibrillation, sepsis).  
  *Rule out immediately:* CT pulmonary angiogram or CT head + thrombophilia screen **before** starting empiric anticoagulation.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Unexplained venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) — especially recurrent or at unusual sites (cerebral sinus, hepatic, renal, portal)
  - Arterial thrombosis in young patient (<50 years) — stroke, TIA, myocardial infarction, limb ischaemia
  - Recurrent early pregnancy loss (≥3, or ≥1 late loss >10 weeks)
  - Premature pre-eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, placental insufficiency
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Signs of DVT (calf swelling, warmth, Homan's sign — though unreliable)
  - Livedo racemosa (reticulated, non-blanching purplish rash on trunk/limbs)
  - Splinter haemorrhages, digital ischaemia, skin ulcers (suggestive of microvascular occlusion)
  - Hypertension ± proteinuria (if renal artery thrombosis or pre-eclampsia)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Inherited thrombophilia** (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation): family history, younger age, usually no pregnancy loss pattern — differentiate with genetic testing.
- **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT):** thrombocytopenia, timing after heparin use, positive serotonin release assay; **splitter:** if on heparin, check platelet count and functional assay.
- **Paradoxical embolism (PFO):** young with cryptogenic stroke — no thrombophilia markers; echo with bubble study.
- **Vasculitis (SLE, Behçet's):** systemic inflammation, oral/genital ulcers, serositis, positive ANCA/ANA — splitter: thrombocytopenia + livedo points to APS; complement low suggests SLE.
- **Malignancy-related thrombosis:** weight loss, anaemia, thrombocytosis, age >50; CT chest/abdo/pelvis if unexplained.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (if chest pain: look for RV strain in PE)
  - Urine dipstick (proteinuria, haematuria)
  - Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age
  - Observe for livedo racemosa
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (thrombocytopenia common), coagulation screen (prolonged aPTT if lupus anticoagulant present), LFTs, U&Es, CRP, ESR
  - Thrombophilia screen: **lupus anticoagulant** (dilute Russell viper venom time, aPTT with mixing studies), **anticardiolipin antibodies** (IgG/IgM), **anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies**
  - *Note:* Warfarin/heparin can interfere with lupus anticoagulant testing; take blood before starting anticoagulation if possible
  - If CAPS suspected: LDH, haptoglobin, schistocytes on blood film (MAHA), troponin, lactate
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Doppler ultrasound for DVT; CT pulmonary angiogram for PE; CT/MR venography for cerebral sinus thrombosis
  - **Second-line:** Echocardiogram (valve lesions, Libman-Sacks endocarditis), MRI brain if neurological symptoms
- **Diagnostic criteria (Revised Sapporo criteria — used for research but guides clinical diagnosis):**
  - **Clinical:** ≥1 episode of thrombosis (any site, any vessel) **or** pregnancy morbidity (≥3 early losses, ≥1 late loss >10 weeks, ≥1 preterm delivery <34 weeks due to pre-eclampsia/placental insufficiency)
  - **Laboratory:** Persistent moderate/high-titre anticardiolipin or anti-β2 glycoprotein I antibodies, or lupus anticoagulant detected on ≥2 occasions at least 12 weeks apart
- **Never-miss test:** **Thrombophilia screen (including lupus anticoagulant) BEFORE starting anticoagulation** — if already on warfarin, test when off (e.g., bridging with LMWH) or check IgG/IgM antibodies as they are less affected.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute thrombosis (DVT/PE): start therapeutic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily or 1 mg/kg BD) — **do not delay for test results** if high suspicion
  - If suspected CAPS: **urgent senior/haematology involvement**, start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily × 3 days), IVIG (1 g/kg daily × 2 days), and consider plasma exchange
  - If stroke/TIA without large vessel occlusion: same LMWH, then transition to warfarin; avoid OCP, smoking
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Confirm diagnosis with repeat antibodies after 12 weeks
  - Start warfarin once LMWH therapeutic (overlap for ≥5 days until INR therapeutic)
  - Target INR 2.0–3.0 for venous thrombosis; **consider target 3.0–4.0 if arterial thrombosis or recurrent events on conventional INR** — discuss with haematologist
  - For pregnancy: low-dose aspirin ± LMWH (avoid warfarin) — refer to obstetric medicine
  - Avoid DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban etc.) in triple-positive APS (lupus anticoagulant + both anti-cardiolipin & anti-β2) — increased thrombotic risk; use warfarin only
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (except single provoked VTE with low-titre antibodies — discuss individualised)
  - Monitor INR weekly until stable, then monthly
  - Address modifiable risk factors: control BP, lipids, avoid OCP/HRT, stop smoking
  - Regular follow-up at rheumatology/haematology clinic
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance if stable; avoid dehydration (prothrombotic)
  - VTE prophylaxis: **not indicated** in APS itself (already on therapeutic anticoagulation); if not yet started — use LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) only if low bleeding risk and no current thrombosis
  - Diet/NBM: nil if stroke with dysphagia, otherwise oral diet
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; more frequent if unstable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **LMWH (enoxaparin):** therapeutic dose 1.5 mg/kg SC daily (or 1 mg/kg BD) — for acute thrombosis
  - **Warfarin:** loading dose 5–10 mg PO daily for 2 days, then adjust according to INR; typical maintenance 2–6 mg daily
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Unfractionated heparin (IV with aPTT monitoring) if LMWH contraindicated (e.g., severe renal impairment)
  - Fondaparinux (if HIT history)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - DOACs: avoid in triple-positive APS (increased risk of stroke); use only if low-titre, single negative lupus anticoagulant — haematology advice
  - NSAIDs + anticoagulation: increased bleeding risk
  - Antibiotics (e.g., metronidazole, fluconazole) potentiate warfarin — check INR frequently
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (1 mg/kg once daily) or switch to unfractionated heparin
  - Warfarin: no renal adjustment; caution in hepatic impairment (check baseline INR)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting warfarin without LMWH cover in acute thrombosis** — risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis (due to protein C depletion) and paradoxical embolism from unstable clot

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Catastrophic APS (CAPS) — mortality 30–50% if untreated
  - Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) — rare but possible; check platelets before heparin and every 2–3 days
  - Bleeding (especially if supratherapeutic INR) — reverse warfarin with vitamin K (1–5 mg PO/IV) or fresh frozen plasma if major bleed
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent thrombosis despite anticoagulation (check compliance, INR variability, consider target INR 3–4)
  - Accelerated atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction
  - Chronic kidney disease from renal artery thrombosis or microangiopathy
  - Cognitive decline (microvascular strokes)
  - Non-thrombotic manifestations: thrombocytopenia, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, valve disease (Libman-Sacks)

### Prognosis
- With appropriate anticoagulation, 5-year survival >90% for uncomplicated APS. Catastrophic APS has 30–50% mortality. Recurrence risk 10–20% at 5 years if INR subtherapeutic. Pregnancy outcomes good with LMWH + aspirin (live birth >80%).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always repeat antibody testing after 12 weeks** to confirm persistent positivity — single positive can be transient (e.g., infection, drug-induced).
- **In pregnancy**, start low-dose aspirin (75 mg daily) before conception, and add LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg daily or BD) once pregnancy confirmed — continue until 6 weeks postpartum.
- **Use the "APS score"** (Global APS Score) to stratify thrombotic risk (combines antibody profile, clinical history) — helps guide intensity of anticoagulation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT prescribe DOACs in triple-positive APS** (lupus anticoagulant + both anticardiolipin and anti-β2 antibodies) — increased risk of stroke.
- **Do NOT wait for antibody results to start anticoagulation** in acute thrombosis with high clinical suspicion — treat first, test later.
- **Do NOT stop warfarin for minor procedures** without haematology advice — bridging with LMWH is often needed because of high thrombotic risk.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening chest pain, shortness of breath, haemoptysis (PE)
  - New severe headache, stroke-like symptoms (cerebral sinus thrombosis)
  - Signs of bleeding (overt haemorrhage, melaena, haematuria, easy bruising)
  - Unexplained fever, confusion, hypotension (possible CAPS)
- **Follow-up:** Haematology clinic within 4–6 weeks for INR check/antibody repeat; rheumatology clinic if associated SLE. Anticoagulation review with community pharmacist (e.g., INR monitoring).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Suspected CAPS (organ failure, MAHA, rapid deterioration)
  - Any thrombosis on anticoagulation (breakthrough event)
  - Major bleeding (haemodynamic instability, fall in Hb >20 g/L)
  - Any pregnant woman with new thrombosis or obstetric complication
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <13 seizures, or focal neurology
  - Massive PE with shock or cardiac arrest
